subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Put your hand on the middle of your chest. Can you feel a soft thump, thump, thump? That is your heart beating right now, and it has been beating every single second since before you were born. Take a slow breath in through your nose. The air you just breathed is filling your lungs. Right now, without you even thinking about it, your body is doing hundreds of amazing jobs at the same time. In this unit you will explore the busy world hidden inside your own body.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Name and point to the major organs of your body: the brain, heart, lungs, stomach, intestines, and skeleton.
Explain the main job of each of these organs in simple, clear words.
Describe how organs work together as teams, or systems, to keep you alive.
Find and measure your own pulse and count your breathing rate at rest and after moving.
Explain how exercise changes your heartbeat and your breathing, and why this happens.
Name several healthy habits - good food, exercise, clean water, cleanliness, and rest - that keep your organs strong.
Use the correct science words such as organ, pulse, oxygen, nutrients, and digest when you talk about your body.
Here are the important words for this unit. You will meet them again and again, so learn them well. Try using each one in a sentence with your family.
A special part of your body that has its own important job.

explorations.title: The Pulse Detective
explorations.context: To find your pulse and discover how your heart rate changes when you rest and when you exercise.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down what you think will happen. Do you think your heart will beat faster or slower after you exercise? Why? Write your idea before you test it.
explorations.answer: Which measurement gave the highest heart rate? How much did your heart rate go up from resting to after exercise? Did your heart rate come back down after you rested? Was your hypothesis correct? Why do you think your heart beats faster when you exercise?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find your pulse and discover how your heart rate changes when you rest and when you exercise.

explorations.title: The Breathing Counter
explorations.context: To measure how many times you breathe in a minute, and to find out how exercise changes your breathing.
explorations.prompt: Do you think you will breathe more times or fewer times per minute after exercise? Write down your prediction.
explorations.answer: When did you breathe the fastest? Why do you think your body needs more air when you exercise? Did your breathing and your heart rate both go up together? What does that tell you about how your organs work as a team?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To measure how many times you breathe in a minute, and to find out how exercise changes your breathing.

explorations.title: Ribs, the Protective Cage
explorations.context: To discover how your skeleton, especially your ribs, protects the soft organs inside your chest.
explorations.prompt: Which soft organs do you think your ribs are protecting? Write your idea.
explorations.answer: Why is it helpful to have hard bones around your heart and lungs? What might happen to these soft organs if you had no rib cage? Which other important organ has a hard bone protecting it? (Hint: think about your head.)
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To discover how your skeleton, especially your ribs, protects the soft organs inside your chest.

explorations.title: The Senses Test
explorations.context: To find out how well your senses gather information and send it to your brain.
explorations.prompt: Which sense do you think helps you most when your eyes are closed? Write your prediction.
explorations.answer: Which sense was easiest to use with your eyes closed? Where do all your senses send their messages? (Hint: it is the control center.) Why is it useful to have five different senses instead of just one?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out how well your senses gather information and send it to your brain.

methods.title: The Pulse Detective
methods.example: To find your pulse and discover how your heart rate changes when you rest and when you exercise.
steps: Sit quietly and rest for two minutes so your heart is calm.
Turn one hand palm up. Place two fingers of your other hand gently on your wrist below your thumb. Feel the soft tapping - that is your pulse.
Count the taps for 15 seconds while watching the clock. Multiply your count by 4 to get beats per minute. Write it in the table as your resting rate.
Now march in place quickly for two minutes, lifting your knees high.
Straight away, find your pulse again and count for 15 seconds. Multiply by 4 and write it as your after exercise rate.

methods.title: The Breathing Counter
methods.example: To measure how many times you breathe in a minute, and to find out how exercise changes your breathing.
steps: Sit quietly and rest for two minutes. Breathe normally - do not try to change your breathing.
Put one hand on your chest. Each time your chest rises and falls counts as one breath.
Count your breaths for 30 seconds, then multiply by 2 to get breaths per minute. Write it as your resting rate.
March in place or do gentle star jumps for two minutes.
Straight away, count your breaths for 30 seconds again. Multiply by 2 and write it as your after exercise rate.

methods.title: Ribs, the Protective Cage
methods.example: To discover how your skeleton, especially your ribs, protects the soft organs inside your chest.
steps: Sit up tall. Gently press your fingertips along the front and sides of your chest.
Feel the hard, curved bones under your skin - these are your ribs. Count how many you can feel on one side.
Notice how the ribs curve around from your front to your back, making a cage shape.
Take a deep breath in and feel how your ribs move outward to make room for your lungs.
Draw a simple picture of your chest and draw the rib cage. Inside it, draw the two organs it protects.

methods.title: The Senses Test
methods.example: To find out how well your senses gather information and send it to your brain.
steps: Sit down and close your eyes. Ask your helper to gently place a safe object in your hands.
Using only your sense of touch, guess what the object is. Say your guess, then open your eyes to check.
Next, keep your eyes closed and ask your helper to hold a food, like a piece of fruit, near your nose. Guess it using only your sense of smell.
Try listening: eyes closed, have your helper make a soft sound somewhere in the room. Point to where the sound came from.
Record which senses helped you guess correctly.

workedExamples.title: Measuring Your Pulse
workedExamples.prompt: Counting your pulse for a whole minute is hard because it beats so fast. Scientists and doctors use a clever shortcut: they count for a shorter time and then do a small sum. Let us learn how, step by step. This tells you your heart rate, which is the number…
workedExamples.solution: The idea: There are 60 seconds in one minute. Instead of counting for all 60 seconds, we count for 15 seconds. Because 15 seconds is one quarter of a minute, we multiply what we counted by 4 to find the whole minute. Worked Example: Amina wanted to find her resting heart rate. She sat quietly, found her pulse, and counted the beats for 15 seconds using a clock. She counted 22 beats. Step 1 - Write down what you counted. Amina counted 22 beats in 15 seconds. Step 2 - Remember the rule. 15 seconds fits into 1 minute…

workedExamples.title: Amina wanted to find her resting heart rate. She sat quietly, found her pulse, and counted the beats for 15 seconds using a clock. She counted 22 beats.
workedExamples.prompt: Step 1 - Write down what you counted. Amina counted 22 beats in 15 seconds.
workedExamples.solution: Step 2 - Remember the rule. 15 seconds fits into 1 minute exactly 4 times, so we multiply by 4. Step 3 - Do the sum. 22 x 4 = 88. Step 4 - Write the answer with the units. Amina's heart rate is 88 beats per minute. Now try one yourself. Hassan counted 20 beats in 15 seconds. What is his heart rate? Do the sum: 20 x 4 = 80 beats per minute. Well done! A resting heart rate for a child your age is usually somewhere between 70 and 110 beats per minute, so both Amina and Hassan are perfectly healthy.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Which organ controls your thinking and memory? a) Heart b) Brain c) Lungs d) Stomach
workedExamples.solution: 1 b) Brain The brain is the control center for thinking and memory.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: What is the main job of your heart? a) To help you breathe b) To break down food c) To pump blood around your body d) To help you think
workedExamples.solution: 2 c) Pump blood around your body The heart is a muscle that pushes blood everywhere.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Which organs help you breathe? a) Heart and brain b) Lungs c) Stomach and intestines d) Brain and lungs
workedExamples.solution: 3 b) Lungs The lungs take in air so you can breathe.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Where can you feel your pulse? a) In your stomach b) In your wrist or neck c) In your lungs d) In your brain
workedExamples.solution: 4 b) In your wrist or neck The pulse is felt where blood vessels are near the skin.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: What happens to your heart rate when you exercise? a) It stays the same b) It gets slower c) It gets faster d) It stops
workedExamples.solution: 5 c) It gets faster Your muscles need more blood and oxygen, so the heart speeds up.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: What is the main job of your stomach? a) To pump blood b) To help you breathe c) To break down food with acid d) To control thinking
workedExamples.solution: 6 c) Break down food with acid The stomach churns food and mixes it with acid.

practice.answer: 1 b) Brain The brain is the control center for thinking and memory.

practice.prompt: What is the main job of your stomach? a) To pump blood b) To help you breathe c) To break down food with acid d) To control thinking

practice.prompt: Which organ soaks up nutrients from your food? a) Heart b) Lungs c) Brain d) Intestines
practice.answer: 7 d) Intestines The intestines soak up nutrients into the blood.

practice.prompt: What does your body take out of the air when you breathe in? a) Water b) Oxygen c) Sand d) Sugar
practice.answer: 8 b) Oxygen Your lungs take oxygen out of the air you breathe in.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What is the job of your skeleton? a) To digest food b) To hold you up and protect your organs c) To pump blood d) To help you smell
practice.answer: 9 b) Hold you up and protect your organs The skeleton is a strong frame that also guards soft organs.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which bones protect your heart and lungs? a) Skull b) Leg bones c) Ribs d) Finger bones
practice.answer: 10 c) Ribs The curved ribs make a cage around the heart and lungs.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: How many senses do you have? a) Two b) Three c) Five d) Ten
practice.answer: 11 c) Five Sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What should you do to keep all your organs healthy? a) Only exercise b) Only sleep c) Only eat d) Exercise, eat well, drink clean water, and rest
practice.answer: 12 d) Exercise, eat well, drink clean water, and rest Good health needs all of these habits together.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: The Pulse Detective
activities.materials: Your own body; A clock or phone with seconds, or a helper to count seconds; Paper and a pencil to record
steps: Sit quietly and rest for two minutes so your heart is calm.
Turn one hand palm up. Place two fingers of your other hand gently on your wrist below your thumb. Feel the soft tapping - that is your pulse.
Count the taps for 15 seconds while watching the clock. Multiply your count by 4 to get beats per minute. Write it in the table as your resting rate.
Now march in place quickly for two minutes, lifting your knees high.
Straight away, find your pulse again and count for 15 seconds. Multiply by 4 and write it as your after exercise rate.

activities.title: The Breathing Counter
activities.materials: Your own body; A clock or phone with seconds, or a helper; Paper and pencil
steps: Sit quietly and rest for two minutes. Breathe normally - do not try to change your breathing.
Put one hand on your chest. Each time your chest rises and falls counts as one breath.
Count your breaths for 30 seconds, then multiply by 2 to get breaths per minute. Write it as your resting rate.
March in place or do gentle star jumps for two minutes.
Straight away, count your breaths for 30 seconds again. Multiply by 2 and write it as your after exercise rate.

activities.title: Ribs, the Protective Cage
activities.materials: Your own body; Paper and pencil to draw
steps: Sit up tall. Gently press your fingertips along the front and sides of your chest.
Feel the hard, curved bones under your skin - these are your ribs. Count how many you can feel on one side.
Notice how the ribs curve around from your front to your back, making a cage shape.
Take a deep breath in and feel how your ribs move outward to make room for your lungs.
Draw a simple picture of your chest and draw the rib cage. Inside it, draw the two organs it protects.

activities.title: The Senses Test
activities.materials: A few safe household objects (a date, a spoon, a cloth, a piece of fruit); A helper; Paper and pencil
steps: Sit down and close your eyes. Ask your helper to gently place a safe object in your hands.
Using only your sense of touch, guess what the object is. Say your guess, then open your eyes to check.
Next, keep your eyes closed and ask your helper to hold a food, like a piece of fruit, near your nose. Guess it using only your sense of smell.
Try listening: eyes closed, have your helper make a soft sound somewhere in the room. Point to where the sound came from.
Record which senses helped you guess correctly.

activities.title: Observe: Keeping Your Organs Healthy
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of keeping your organs healthy around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Science Words for the Body
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of science words for the body around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: Organ
A special part of your body with its own important job.
Brain
The control center inside your head; it thinks and controls the body.
Heart
The muscle that pumps blood around your body, day and night.
Lungs
The two organs in your chest that take in oxygen when you breathe.
Stomach
The organ that breaks food down with acid after you swallow.
Intestines
Long tubes that soak up nutrients from food into the blood.
Skeleton
All your bones joined together; holds you up and protects organs.
Pulse
The thump of blood you feel each time your heart beats.
Oxygen
A gas in the air that every part of your body needs.
Nutrients
The goodness in food that gives energy and helps you grow.
Digest
To break food down so your body can use it.
Senses
Sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch.

vocabulary.meaning: A special part of your body with its own important job.
vocabulary.example: Use the correct science words such as organ, pulse, oxygen, nutrients, and digest when you talk about your body.

vocabulary.meaning: The control center inside your head; it thinks and controls the body.
vocabulary.example: Your heart, your lungs, your brain, your stomach, and many others are all organs, and they all work together as one team to keep you alive, strong, and able to run and play.

vocabulary.meaning: The muscle that pumps blood around your body, day and night.
vocabulary.example: That is your heart beating right now, and it has been beating every single second since before you were born.

vocabulary.meaning: The two organs in your chest that take in oxygen when you breathe.
vocabulary.example: The air you just breathed is filling your lungs.

vocabulary.meaning: The organ that breaks food down with acid after you swallow.
vocabulary.example: Your heart, your lungs, your brain, your stomach, and many others are all organs, and they all work together as one team to keep you alive, strong, and able to run and play.

vocabulary.meaning: Long tubes that soak up nutrients from food into the blood.
vocabulary.example: Nutrients from fish pass through your intestines.

vocabulary.meaning: All your bones joined together; holds you up and protects organs.
vocabulary.example: All the bones in your body join together to make your skeleton.

vocabulary.meaning: The thump of blood you feel each time your heart beats.
vocabulary.example: Find and measure your own pulse and count your breathing rate at rest and after moving.

vocabulary.meaning: A gas in the air that every part of your body needs.
vocabulary.example: Use the correct science words such as organ, pulse, oxygen, nutrients, and digest when you talk about your body.

vocabulary.meaning: The goodness in food that gives energy and helps you grow.
vocabulary.example: Use the correct science words such as organ, pulse, oxygen, nutrients, and digest when you talk about your body.

vocabulary.meaning: To break food down so your body can use it.
vocabulary.example: Use the correct science words such as organ, pulse, oxygen, nutrients, and digest when you talk about your body.

vocabulary.meaning: Sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch.
vocabulary.example: Your senses tell your brain about the sea breeze.

commonMistakes: The heart is on the far left of the chest.
It is only tilted slightly left.
Food travels into your arms and legs.
Food stays in the digestive organs.
The brain rests when you sleep.
We breathe just to fill our belly.

connections.text: living things breathe, eat, and grow - your organs are how your body does these things.
camels, goats, and other animals also have hearts, lungs, and stomachs that do the same jobs.
cleanliness, clean water, and healthy food keep your organs strong.
you use multiplication when you work out your heart rate and breathing rate.
caring for your body is caring for a gift, and cleanliness is part of faith.
Measure the resting heart rates of your whole family and compare them.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you have learned to answer these real-life questions.
realProblems.answer: 1 Her muscles needed more oxygen, so her lungs breathed faster to take in air and her heart beat faster to deliver oxygen-rich blood to her legs.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1 Her muscles needed more oxygen, so her lungs breathed faster to take in air and her heart beat faster to deliver oxygen-rich blood to her legs.

realProblems.prompt: Halima ran to the well and back. She felt her heart pounding and she was breathing hard. Explain why both her heart and her lungs were working harder.
realProblems.answer: 2 Example: Fish and camel milk give nutrients and help build strong bones and muscles; dates and fruit give energy and vitamins. Any sensible link is correct.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2 Example: Fish and camel milk give nutrients and help build strong bones and muscles; dates and fruit give energy and vitamins. Any sensible link is correct.

realProblems.prompt: Omar's mother gives him camel milk, fish, dates, and fruit. Choose one of these foods and explain how it helps his body.
realProblems.answer: 3 Any two: rest, drink clean water, eat a healthy snack like dates or fruit.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3 Any two: rest, drink clean water, eat a healthy snack like dates or fruit.

realProblems.prompt: After playing football, Idris felt tired and thirsty. Name two healthy things he should do to look after his body.
realProblems.answer: 4 Any two: eat healthy foods with calcium like milk, play and exercise, spend time in the sunshine, drink milk.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 4 Any two: eat healthy foods with calcium like milk, play and exercise, spend time in the sunshine, drink milk.

realProblems.prompt: Zahra wants to keep her bones strong. Suggest two things she can do.
realProblems.answer: 5 Smoky or dusty air is bad for the lungs; clean, fresh air keeps the lungs healthy so we can breathe well.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5 Smoky or dusty air is bad for the lungs; clean, fresh air keeps the lungs healthy so we can breathe well.

realProblems.prompt: Explain to a younger child why we should not breathe smoky, dusty air if we can avoid it.
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name three organs in your body and give one job for each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1 Any three, such as: brain (controls the body), heart (pumps blood), lungs (help you breathe), stomach (breaks down food), intestines (soak up nutrients).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is an organ? Explain in your own words.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2 An organ is a special part of your body that has its own important job.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does your heart beat faster when you run and play football?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3 Running muscles need more oxygen and food, so the heart beats faster to deliver more blood.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What do your lungs do when you breathe in?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4 The lungs fill with air and take oxygen out of it, passing the oxygen into the blood.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What happens to a plate of bariis after you swallow it? Name the two organs that help.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5 It goes to the stomach, which breaks it down with acid, then to the intestines, which soak up the nutrients.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why is your skeleton important? Give two reasons.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6 Any two: it holds you up and gives you shape; it protects soft organs; it lets you move at your joints.

questions.question: Which organ controls your thinking and memory?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Brain The brain is the control center for thinking and memory.

questions.question: What is the main job of your heart?
options: To pump blood around your body
questions.answer: To pump blood around your body
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Pump blood around your body The heart is a muscle that pushes blood everywhere.

questions.question: Which organs help you breathe?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Lungs The lungs take in air so you can breathe.

questions.question: Where can you feel your pulse?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: In your wrist or neck The pulse is felt where blood vessels are near the skin.

questions.question: What happens to your heart rate when you exercise?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: It gets faster Your muscles need more blood and oxygen, so the heart speeds up.

questions.question: What is the main job of your stomach?
options: To break down food with acid
questions.answer: To break down food with acid
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Break down food with acid The stomach churns food and mixes it with acid.

questions.question: Which organ soaks up nutrients from your food?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Intestines The intestines soak up nutrients into the blood.

questions.question: What does your body take out of the air when you breathe in?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Oxygen Your lungs take oxygen out of the air you breathe in.

questions.question: What is the job of your skeleton?
options: To hold you up and protect your organs
questions.answer: To hold you up and protect your organs
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Hold you up and protect your organs The skeleton is a strong frame that also guards soft organs.

questions.question: Which bones protect your heart and lungs?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Ribs The curved ribs make a cage around the heart and lungs.

questions.question: How many senses do you have?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Five Sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch.

questions.question: What should you do to keep all your organs healthy?
options: Exercise, eat well, drink clean water, and rest
questions.answer: Exercise, eat well, drink clean water, and rest
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Exercise, eat well, drink clean water, and rest Good health needs all of these habits together.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to:
I can name and point to the major organs of your body: the brain, heart, lungs, stomach, intestines, and skeleton.
I can explain the main job of each of these organs in simple, clear words.
I can describe how organs work together as teams, or systems, to keep you alive.
I can find and measure your own pulse and count your breathing rate at rest and after moving.
I can explain how exercise changes your heartbeat and your breathing, and why this happens.
I can name several healthy habits - good food, exercise, clean water, cleanliness, and rest - that keep your organs strong.
I can use the correct science words such as organ, pulse, oxygen, nutrients, and digest when you talk about your body.
